##section:executive_summary
The excavation of mock_evaluation revealed a discontinuous series of cultural deposits surrounded by an underlying natural Late Pleistocene deposit, with modern artefacts concentrated within the primary fill that was inadvertently preserved under a contemporary topsoil horizon. All contexts were recorded and bulk specimens were collected for flotation analysis, yielding a total of 44 items that span Roman, Romano‑British, post‑medieval and contemporary periods. The stratigraphic arrangement indicates that the linear cut (context [102]) cut through a naturally occurring layer (context [105]) and was subsequently filled with a primary fill of dark greyish‑black silty clay (Roman) that was overlain by a secondary fill of mid‑brown clayey silt (also Roman). The overlying modern post‑medieval fill (context [101]) is described as a filled, linear feature.

##section:introduction
The site is located within the historic_england_cl3 jurisdiction and its exact north‑grid reference number (NGR) is not provided in the supplied data. Geological surveys indicate the location is on a gently undulating surface that hosts a series of anthropogenic and natural stratigraphic layers. The project was carried out under the mock evaluation programme to catalogue and evaluate potential heritage resources on the site.

##section:methodology
Archaeological layers were identified by visual and contextual observation and recorded using a systematic stratigraphic notation system. Excavation involved the manual removal of topsoil and the construction of a rectangular section to expose the linear cut (context [102]) and associated fills. All finds were catalogued with an identification code, type and approximate size. Sampling was planned for bulk soil flotation, with two samples taken from contexts [103] and [104] for sedimentological and palaeontological evaluation. The finds summary and environmental sampling results are recorded in the accompanying tables.

##section:stratigraphic_narrative
From the lowest level to the highest, the sequence comprises: the natural Late Pleistocene deposit (context [105]; weathered natural, head deposit) that is cut by the linear feature (context [102]); the primary fill of the cut (context [104]; dark greyish‑black silty clay, frequent charcoal inclusions, moderate sub‑angular flint; Roman); the secondary fill placed within the cut (context [103]; mid‑brown clayey silt, occasional charcoal fragments, occasional fired clay; Roman) that fills the space left by the primary fill; and the overlying filled topsoil layer (context [101]; dark greyish‑brown silty loam, moderate frequency sub‑angular flint; modern that is filled by the cut). No dating evidence has been recovered from the cut itself beyond the cultural assignments supplied in the context register.

##section:finds_summary
Foundry debris is recorded for each period. In the Roman principal deposit (context [104]) 5 pottery sherds, 8 animal bone fragments, 6 fired‑clay fragments and 0 pottery were recovered. The secondary deposit (context [103]) yielded 12 pottery sherds, 8 animal bone fragments and 0 pottery. The modern topsoil deposit (context [101]) comprises 3 pottery sherds, 5 CBM (5 fragments of a roof tile), 2 green bottle‑type glass artefacts and 0 animal bone. No further details are available for the Roman primary deposit (context [104]) beyond the sherd counts noted.

##section:environmental_summary
Two bulk samples were taken for flotation analysis: SAMP001 represents bulk soil from the primary fill (dark greyish‑black silty clay, Roman) and SAMP002 represents bulk soil from the secondary fill (mid‑brown clayey silt, Roman). No additional data are available regarding composition or dating implications of the samples.

##section:discussion
The chronological juxtaposition of Romano‑British potentiality (cutting of context [102]) and subsequent Roman occupations (contexts [104] and [103]) suggests a settlement phase during the Roman period that was later disturbed by later Romano‑British activity. The presence of modern artefacts within the Roman primary fill indicates a later land‑cover event, likely related to land reclamation or drainage. The mixed nature of the finds (Roman and post‑medieval) requires caution in attributing specific periods to the linear features. No evidence of a later cultural activity beyond the modern topsoil fill was recovered.

##section:archive_statement
The full excavation record, including contextual notes, photographic documentation, find details and sample analyses, should be deposited in the local authority heritage record at accession number mock_evalu_doc2026_05_24_2031. All subsequent reproductions must retain this original accession identifier.
